one_line_takeaway: BlueSuffix is a black-box VLM jailbreak defense that chains a diffusion image purifier, an LLM text purifier, and a PPO-fine-tuned GPT-2 'defensive suffix' generator to cut cross-modal attack success rates by ~50-70% (and to 0% on unimodal attacks) while barely touching benign accuracy.
key_finding: On image-based (VAA, imgJP) and text-based (GCG, AutoDAN) attacks, BlueSuffix drove ASR to 0.00% in every case across all tested VLMs. On the harder bimodal BAP attack (MM-SafetyBench) it cut ASR by 56.37% on LLaVA (61.02->4.65%), 52.89% on MiniGPT-4 (62.26->9.37%), and 51.70% on InstructionBLIP (58.48->6.78%), beating the best unimodal baseline by at least 12-23 percentage points. It transferred to unseen RedTeam-2K jailbreaks (LLaVA BAP 80.20->7.05%, a 73.15-point drop) and to the commercial black-box Gemini-1.5-flash (BAP ASR from ~50% down to ~2.5%). It stayed robust under a fully-informed adaptive BAP attack, which raised ASR by under 1 point (4.65%->5.64% on LLaVA). Benign utility was largely preserved (BPR 78.00% vs 81.60% for original prompts, a 3.60-point drop).
core_contribution: BlueSuffix is a black-box, plug-and-play 'blue-team' defense that protects Vision-Language Models (VLMs) against cross-modal jailbreak attacks without accessing target-model parameters and without meaningfully degrading benign performance. Its own novelty is the combination of three components into a single pipeline: (1) a diffusion-based image purifier (reused from DiffPure) to strip adversarial visual perturbations, (2) an LLM-based text purifier (GPT-4o or Llama-3-8B-Instruct) that rewrites the malicious textual prompt into a more detailed form without changing its meaning, and (3) the paper's central technical novelty, a lightweight GPT-2-based 'blue-team suffix generator' fine-tuned with reinforcement learning (PPO) to append a defensive suffix to the purified text. The RL reward is a binary safety score (1 benign / 0 harmful) from an LLM judge on the target VLM's actual response, so the reward implicitly captures both image and text modalities, giving cross-modal robustness. Unlike prior bimodal defenses (Jailguard, CIDER) that DETECT and reject malicious inputs, BlueSuffix does not detect; it purifies and reinforces the prompt so the VLM itself recognizes and safely answers. Note: Jailguard, CIDER, DiffPure, and the various attacks are cited prior work, not this paper's contribution; BlueSuffix reuses DiffPure as its image purifier.
threat_model: Black-box defense setting: the defender has NO access to the target VLM's internal structure or parameters, only its inputs and outputs, enabling plug-and-play deployment across different VLMs and API services. The defender gets a one-shot opportunity to sanitize each potentially-jailbroken input while preserving benign-input performance. Attackers craft multimodal jailbreak prompts (image + text) secretly and independently, possibly mixed with benign queries, aiming to maximize the target VLM's log-likelihood of producing harmful content. The paper also evaluates a strong ADAPTIVE attacker who knows all defense components (including the fine-tuned suffix generator) and re-runs the BAP attack against the purified prompts.
limitations: The image purifier is entirely reused DiffPure, so the genuine methodological novelty is narrow (the RL-trained suffix generator plus the pipeline composition). The suffix generator was trained on a single attack (BAP) and a single model's responses (LLaVA), so although transfer results are strong, all evaluation shares the same BAP/MM-SafetyBench lineage for the hard cases. The pipeline depends heavily on powerful external LLMs (GPT-4o as text purifier AND as reward judge AND as the evaluation judge), which risks circularity between the reward signal and the reported ASR metric, and adds inference cost/API dependence at deployment. Reported perfect 0.00% ASR on four attack types is unusually clean and offers little headroom to distinguish method quality. Benign performance still drops 3.6 points, and the ablation notes that the text purifier used alone can occasionally strengthen jailbreaks. The mutual-information cross-modal evidence is thin (only 50 pairs, and the suffix raises MI marginally from 4.3667 to 4.4728). Adaptive-attack evaluation covers one adaptive strategy (re-running BAP), not gradient-based or purifier-aware attacks.

datasets_used: MM-SafetyBench (13 jailbreak topics; used for training the suffix generator and for BAP evaluation)
AdvBench (used with ImgJP, GCG, AutoDAN attacks)
AlpacaEval (500 benign prompts for Benign Passing Rate)
quantitative_results: BAP on LLaVA-v1.5-7B: ASR 61.02% (no defense) -> 4.65% (BlueSuffix), a 56.37-point reduction
VAA, ImgJP, GCG, AutoDAN: BlueSuffix reduced ASR to 0.00% in all reported cases
RedTeam-2K transfer, BAP on LLaVA: 80.20% -> 7.05% (73.15-point drop); MiniGPT-4 82.20% -> 14.90%; InstructionBLIP 77.05% -> 9.60%
Benign Passing Rate: BlueSuffix 78.00% vs 81.60% original prompts (3.60-point drop); DiffPure 78.80%, Safety Prompt 74.80%, DiffPure+Safety Prompt 74.00%
Adaptive BAP on LLaVA (MM-SafetyBench): 4.65% -> 5.64% average ASR (<1-point increase)
Mutual information (KNN, 50 pairs): Image 1.4093, Text 2.7756, Image+Text 4.3667, Image+Text+Suffix 4.4728
Training: PPO fine-tuning of GPT-2, batch size 32, stop when expected safety score > 0.95 (~300 epochs)
Headline abstract claim: ~70% ASR reduction on open-source VLMs and ~50% on commercial VLMs against a state-of-the-art attack
baselines_compared: Safety Prompt (safety-driven system prompt, unimodal textual)
R2D2 (Robust Refusal Dynamic Defense, white-box, open-source models only)
CAT (Continuous Adversarial Training, white-box, open-source models only)
